coredata.dc:title: Applicability of AuNPs@N-GQDs nanocomposite in the modeling of the amplified electrochemical Ibuprofen aptasensing assay by monitoring of riboflavin
coredata.dc:creator: Roushani, Mahmoud
Shahdost-fard, Faezeh
coredata.dc:description: Here, an ultrasensitive and low-cost electrochemical aptasensing assay is developed based on the applicability of a fabricated nanocomposite from nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs). A modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE) with the AuNPs@N-GQDs nanocomposite (AuNPs@N-GQDs/GCE) as an efficient platform has some unique properties such as high surface area and electrical conductivity. Furthermore, the prepared platform is capable of more loading of aptamer (Apt) molecules as a biological recognition element of Ibuprofen (IBP) on the modified electrode surface. It is noteworthy that in this study, riboflavin (RF) as a universal green probe is used for the first time for electrochemical detection of IBP. According to the proposed strategy and under the optimum condition, the unprecedented detection limit (LOD) of this assay (33.33 aM) is lower than previously reported analytical methods. The results demonstrate the ability of the nanocomposite for designing of the aptasensor, integrated within the electrode format, to cheaper and simpler detection of the IBP with a specificity and sensitivity sufficient for analysis in real samples. It seems that the proposed strategy based on the AuNPs@N-GQDs nanocomposite can be expanded to other nanomaterials. So, this is expected to have promising implications in the design of electrochemical sensors or biosensors for the detection of various targets.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots
Gold nanoparticles
Ibuprofen
Riboflavin
Aptasensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of the N-GQDs and the GQDs
Characterization of the synthesized N-GQDs and GQDs
Characterization of the morphology of the prepared AuNPs@N-GQDs/GCE
Electrochemical study of the prepared AuNPs@N-GQDs/GCE as the designed aptasensor
Comparison between the efficiency of the N-GQDs and GQDs in preparation of the proposed nanocomposite
Quantitative and qualitative measurements of the IBP using the designed aptasensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FT-IR spectra related to CA as a primary substance (A), synthesized GQDs (B) and synthesized N-GQDs (C).

TEM images related to synthesized the GQD (A) and N-GQDs (B), UV–vis spectra of the synthesized GQD (a) at λ = 356 nm with intensity of 0.607 and N-GQD (b) at λ = 339 nm with intensity of 0.823 (C), fluorescence spectra of the synthesized GQD(a) at λ = 564 nm with intensity of 248 and N-GQD (a) at λ = 448 nm with intensity of 466 (D), SEM images related to bare GCE (E), N-GQDs/GCE (F) and AuNPs/N-GQDs/GCE (G).

(A) Recorded Nyquist curves and (B) CVs of the different modified electrodes GCE (a), N-GQDs/GCE (b), AuNPs/N-GQDs/GCE (c), ssDNA1/AuNPs/N-GQDs/GCE (d), ssDNA2/ssDNA1/AuNPs/N-GQDs/GCE (e) and IBP/ssDNA2/ssDNA1/AuNPs/N-GQDs/GCE (f) in the proposed aptasensing strategy in ferro/ferricyanide solution and (C) recorded CVs of the different modified electrodes in RF solution. Inset of part A is the equivalent circuit.
Investigation of electrodeposition of the AuNPs on the surface of GQD/GCE and N-GQD/GCE: GCE (a), N-GQDs/GCE (b), GQDs/GCE (c), AuNPs/GQDs/GCE (d) and AuNPs/N-GQDs/GCE (e).

Recorded DPV signals in 1 mM RF solution and 1 mM NaCl as an electrolyte for the developed aptasensor (A) at different IBP concentrations from a to i: 0 fM, 0.1 fM, 0.1 pM, 10 pM, 50 pM, 0.1 nM, 50 nM, 0.1 μM and 0.2 μM. Inset is the calibration plot of peak current vs. IBP concentration. The linear regression equations: ΔI (μA) = 5.387 [IBP] (nM) + 0.273 (R2 = 0.992) for low concentration and ΔI (μA) = 0.0017 [IBP] (nM) + 0.273 (R2 = 0.9974) for high concentrations. (B) Recorded DPV signals for investigation of specificity of the proposed strategy for prepared electrode (a) and some common drugs with the concentration of 50 nM such as (b) DCF, (c) COD, (d) APAP, (e) valproate sodium and (f) 50 pM of IBP. The electrolyte is the same as part (A).
Electron transfer mechanism of the Rf.
Schematic representation of the electrochemical aptasensing assay for IBP detection.
The R ct and ∆E = E pa – E pc values related to the modified electrodes in the [Fe(CN)6]4−/3− solution.
A comparison of the response characteristics of different sensors for IBP detection.

row.entry: Aptasensor (PAMAM Den c /CdTe QDs d bioconjugate)
Aptasensor (MWCNTs e /IL f /Chit g nanocomposite and MB h )
Aptasensor (AuNPs/N-GQD i nanocomposite by electrochemical oxidation of RF j )

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Reverse Phase High Performance Liquid Chromatography.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Investigation of the IBP detection in some real sample such as pharmaceutical formulation, human blood serum and waste water containing different concentrations of IBP by developed aptasensor.

head.ce:title: Applicability of AuNPs@N-GQDs nanocomposite in the modeling of the amplified electrochemical Ibuprofen aptasensing assay by monitoring of riboflavin

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Ilam, Ilam, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here, an ultrasensitive and low-cost electrochemical aptasensing assay is developed based on the applicability of a fabricated nanocomposite from nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs). A modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE) with the AuNPs@N-GQDs nanocomposite (AuNPs@N-GQDs/GCE) as an efficient platform has some unique properties such as high surface area and electrical conductivity. Furthermore, the prepared platform is capable of more loading of aptamer (Apt) molecules as a biological recognition element of Ibuprofen (IBP) on the modified electrode surface. It is noteworthy that in this study, riboflavin (RF) as a universal green probe is used for the first time for electrochemical detection of IBP. According to the proposed strategy and under the optimum condition, the unprecedented detection limit (LOD) of this assay (33.33 aM) is lower than previously reported analytical methods. The results demonstrate the ability of the nanocomposite for designing of the aptasensor, integrated within the electrode format, to cheaper and simpler detection of the IBP with a specificity and sensitivity sufficient for analysis in real samples. It seems that the proposed strategy based on the AuNPs@N-GQDs nanocomposite can be expanded to other nanomaterials. So, this is expected to have promising implications in the design of electrochemical sensors or biosensors for the detection of various targets.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, nanomaterials such as nanoparticles, nanowires, nanotubes, and quantum dots have been used in various applications. This is owed to the unique characteristics including high surface to volume ratio, conductivity, elasticity and tensile strength, high chemical, and thermal stability which allows them for their use as a functional component in the fabrication of specific devices [1–3]. Among the vast gamut of nanomaterials, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as a new type of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials have ignited much interest in bioimaging, ion detection, photocatalysis and electrochemical luminescence applications [4–6]. This class of the nanomaterials with excellent characteristics such as superior mechanical and electrical properties, high surface area and high surface grafting by using the π-π conjugated network or abundant surface functional groups provides an extraordinary platform for preparing the nanocomposites [7,8]. Furthermore, doping carbon nanomaterials with heteroatoms can effectively tune their intrinsic properties such as electronic, surface and local chemical features [9]. The nitrogen atom has a comparable atomic size to carbon and can provide five valence electrons to bond with carbon atoms. This feature has been widely used for chemical doping of carbon materials [10]. Nitrogen-doped GQDs (N-GQDs) with unique physical and chemical properties has been employed in fluorescence sensing, electrocatalysis and electrochemiluminescence applications [8,11]. N-GQDs's excellent characteristics along with other nanomaterials can be made an attractive matrix as an efficient platform in fabrication of sensing devices. Utilizing the N-GQDs as a nanocomposite is the least represented in electrochemical sensors but, this may be a unique platform for modeling of the electrochemical aptasensors.

Ibuprofen (IBP) as a nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) is extensively used to treat pain and reduce inflammation by decreasing the value of some hormones. Finding a highly sensitive diagnostic technique for IBP detection has challenged scientists because this drug can increase the risk of fatal heart attack or stroke, especially if it uses long-term or take high doses [12,13]. Because of the medical importance of this drug in the attempt to suicide with overdosage and since the IBP is a major component of hospital discharge, trace detection of IBP in real samples is essential. Beyond the demanding efforts, some methods for IBP detection are still at an early stage of development and as such their use in real samples has not been completely evaluated. The only efforts made to exploit the potential utility of nanomaterial in sensing methods for IBP detection are sensors based on aptamer (Apt) molecule, aptasensor. The Apt molecules possess high recognition ability to detect various targets and they have been commonly utilized to construct aptasensors in cooperation with thermal, mass, optical and electrochemical transducers [14,15]. Recently, IBP detection based on aptasensing strategy has reported by our previous investigations [16]. In this study, ultrasensitive electrochemical aptasensing of IBP is improved by using a combination of gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) and N-GQDs as an efficient nanocomposite with specific characteristics by the help of the electrochemical oxidation of riboflavin (RF) as a redox probe. The applicability of the GQDs and N-GQDs as an efficient sub-layer is investigated and the N-GQDs with more advantages is used in this strategy. In Comparison with other aptasensors in IBP detection, using the AuNPs@N-GQDs nanocomposite as an efficient layer and the RF as a redox probe showed an enhanced signal amplification for the IBP detection. One of the important advantages of this study is utilizing the RF as a green probe. The RF (6,7-dimethyl-9-(d-1-ribityl)-isoalloxazine) which is known as vitamin B2 is an essential precursor of two coenzymes including flavin mononucleotide and flavin adenosine dinucleotide. Based on the studied electrochemical behavior of these coenzymes in the literature [17, 18] by accepting and donating two electrons in the isoalloxazine ring, they have reversible two-electron reductions and oxidation waves which both of them are results of overlapping one-electron waves. Electron transfer mechanism of the RF is shown in Scheme 1 . In compare with some common redox probes such as ferro/ferricyanide (Fe(CN)6 4−/3−), rutin (RU) and ferrocene, RF is superior because this is oxidized in negative potential range, where the number of interfering species in sensing is much fewer [16]. It is worthy that, an application of facile AuNPs@N-GQDs nanocomposite and the RF as a green probe for the low-cost detection of IBP has never been reported before.

Analytical grade IBP, urea, citric acid (CA), tetrachloroauric acid (HAuCl4), codeine (COD), sodium valproate, sodium diclofenac (DCF), acetaminophen (APAP) and all other reagents were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, Merck or Fluka chemical companies. The HPLC-purified sequences of the 3′-NH2 capture probe (5′-ATAAGCTGGTAT-3′) and the IBP specific Apt sequence (5′-ATACCAGCTTATTCAATTCCACAGACCCTTAGCTTTCCTATTATTCTG CGCGACGC TGAGATAGTAAGTGCAATCT-3′) were ordered from Bioneer Co, South Korea [16]. Since of the 76-mer Apt sequence is long and it may fold on the electrode surface with unsuitable configuration, the sequence was designed to hybridize with the 12-base capture probe and specifically recognize IBP. This strategy is based on our previous study [14].

The absorption measurement was investigated using an ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) spectrophotometer (VARIAN 300Bio CARY) in dual beam mode with a 1 cm quartz cuvette. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) measurement was performed on a Vertex 70 instrument. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were obtained using an SEM apparatus (Philip's Company, Netherlands). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image was recorded by a Hitachi H-800 electron microscope. The fluorescence spectra were obtained via a Carry Eclipse (Agilent, Fluorescence, and Phosphorescence spectrometer).

All measurements were carried out with a μ-AUTOLAB electrochemical system type III and FRA2 board computer controlled Potentiostat/Galvanostat (Eco-Chemie, Switzerland) driven with NOVA software. A conventional three-electrode system containing a GCE as a working electrode, a platinum wire as a counter electrode and Ag/AgCl (3 M KCl) as a reference electrode were employed. Differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) and cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements were performed in 0.1 M phosphate buffer (BP) solution (pH = 7.4) containing 1 mM RF solution and 1 mM NaCl as an electrolyte by scanning a potential of −0.2 to −0.8 V with modulation amplitude of 25 mV, modulation time of 50 ms and scan rate of 100 mVs−1. Also, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurement for confirming of a layer by layer immobilization of the substrates was recorded at a frequency range from 0.1 to 100 kHz in 0.1 M KCl solution containing 5 mM Fe(CN)6 4−/3−. All measurements were carried out at room temperature.

A pharmaceutical formulation, human blood serum, and waste-water were used as real samples. To measure the IBP in the pill, a packet of IBP pills was purchased from the local drugstore and was powdered. A certain gram of the obtained powder was dissolved in the certain volume of distillated water. Furthermore, for determination of the IBP in the waste-water, 100 μL IBP (1 nM) was spiked to the certain volume of the waste-water sample. Also, centrifuged healthy people's serum was supplied by a local hospital and 100 μL IBP (1 nM) was spiked to the certain volume of the prepared serum samples. Finally, 10 μL of each prepared solutions were separately and independently dropped on the surface of the prepared aptasensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the N-GQDs and the GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Using a specific sub-layer as an efficient platform for immobilization of the further substrates is an essential step in the fabrication of each sensor. In this study, the effect of both of the N-GQDs and GQDs as a sub-layer in preparation of a suitable nanocomposite in the modeling of the aptasensor is investigated. The N-GQDs was synthesized by referring to the reported methods in the literature and making some modifications to them [19]. Briefly, 0.525 g of CA and 0.45 g of urea were added to 12 mL of water and stirred for 10 min. Then, the obtained mixture was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon lined stainless autoclave and heated to 160 °C in an oven (8 h). Afterward, the final product was cooled to room temperature and collected by adding ethanol into the solution. Finally, the obtained solution was centrifuged at 5000 rpm (20 min) and stored in a brown glass bottle in the refrigerator.

The synthesis of the GQDs sample was prepared by directly pyrolyzing of CA [20]. According to the literature and with some modification, 2.0 g of CA was heated to 200 °C and after 5 min, liquated CA was achieved. Subsequently, the color of the liquated CA was changed from colorless to pale yellow and after 30 min, the CA with orange color was achieved. The color change from colorless to orange color confirms the successful synthesis of the GQDs. Then the resultant GQDs was added into NaOH solution (100 mL of 10 mg mL−1) under vigorous stirring to obtain neutralized GQDs with pH = 7.0. Finally, the colloidal solution of the GQDs without any precipitate was stored in a brown glass bottle in the refrigerator.

It is noteworthy that the chemical synthesis of the N-GQDs and GQDs with the described methods is a simple and economical process which can be applied elsewhere and this is another advantage of the proposed strategy.

The fabrication process for the proposed aptasensor is shown in Scheme 2 . According to this strategy, to GCE pretreatment, a bare GCE was polished using alumina slurry (0.05 μM) on micro-cloth pads, rinsed thoroughly with ultrapure distillated water and dried before use. The synthesized N-GQDs solution was sonicated for 10 min to obtain a homogenous solution. Then, 5 μL of this solution was dropped onto the surface of freshly polished GCE and dried at room temperature. The obtained N-GQDs/GCE was immersed in a solution which was 1.3 mM in HAuCl4 as the gold precursor and 0.5 M in H2SO4 [20-22]. By applying a potential range between 0.2 and 1.5 V for 5 cycles with a scan rate of 10 mVs−1, a layer of AuNPs was electrodeposited on the N-GQDs/GCE surface and the AuNPs@N-GQDs/GCE as the modified electrode was achieved. In the next step, 5 μL of 5 μM amino capture probe (NH2-ssDNA1) was dropped on the obtained AuNPs@N-GQDs/GCE for 5 h at 4 °C. Then the electrode was washed several times with PB solution to remove the unbound Apt molecules. In this way, the NH2-ssDNA1 was immobilized through the formation of covalent bond between the AuNPs and amino groups of 3′ end of the capture probe. After that, 5 μL of 5 μM IBP specific Apt (ssDNA2) was incubated on the ssDNA1/AuNPs@N-GQDs/GCE surface for 4 h at 4 °C [16]. It was then thoroughly rinsed with PB and dried with nitrogen gas and then stored in the BP solution at 4 °C when not in use. To ensure that each substrate was attached on the surface of the modified GCE, EIS and CV techniques as the measurement technique were applied and furthermore, the SEM images from the modified electrode surface were recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the synthesized N-GQDs and GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: For confirming of the synthesized N-GQDs which is functionalized with N atoms, the FT-IR spectra of CA, GQDs and N-GQDs were recorded (Fig. 1 ). By comparing three spectra in Fig. 1, this is obvious that each absorbance bands in these spectra are different in shape, the intensity of transmittance and wavenumber value. As can be seen in spectrum B, the presence of absorbance bands with sharp peaks at 1288.18 cm−1, 1573.42 cm−1, and 1391.15 cm−1 are ascribed to the C-OH and COO stretching vibrations related to OH and COOH groups, respectively. This is indicating pyrolysis product of the CA has carboxylic acid groups. It may be an appropriate proof for the successful synthesis of the GQDs. Furthermore, in spectra B, no characteristic absorption bands of aromatic compounds such as skeletal vibration of aromatic rings (~1450–1650 cm−1) and stretching vibration of CH in the aromatic ring (~3000–3100 cm−1) are observed. It can be confirmed that there are not aromatic compounds in the synthesized GQDs. In spectra C, characteristics of the absorption peaks about 3023 cm−1 to 3200 cm−1, 1639 cm−1, and 1399 cm−1 indicating the existence of the stretching vibrations of NH, CN, and CN groups, respectively, which are confirming doping of the N atoms in the synthesized N-GQDs. Also, it seems that existence of some absorbance bands for carbonyl and carboxyl groups around 1399 cm−1 and 1639 cm−1 are covered with broad bands of CN and CN groups, respectively. The obtained results were in line with the findings of previous studies [20,23].

TEM technique was used to confirm the nanostructure synthesis of the GQDs and the N-GQDs. The TEM images in Fig. 2A and B corroborate synthesis of these nanoparticles with nanosize structure, which could serve as a unique modifier for increasing of the modified electrode surface area. Furthermore, UV–vis spectra of the synthesized N-GQDs and the GQDs were studied. As shown in Fig. 2C, the N-GQDs has an absorbance intensity of 0.823 while the GQDs shows lower absorbance intensity (0.607). Furthermore, in comparison to the GQDs, the N-GQDs shows considerably stronger absorbance intensity and its peak position is about 339 nm, which is blue-shifted from the GQDs (356 nm). Also, investigation of fluorescence spectra of the synthesized N-GQDs and the GQDs in Fig. 2D indicating a maximum intensity of 564 and 248 which are located at 448 nm and 466 nm, respectively (430 nm was recorded as an excitation wavelength). These results are in agreement with other reported in the literature [24]. The blue shift and the intensity increase in the peak of the N-GQDs may attribute to the fact that incorporation of the heteroatoms in the graphene lattice could disrupt sp2 hybridization of carbon atoms. So, since of that the GQDs and N-GQDs were synthesized from the same substrate (CA), change in the electronic structure of the N-GQDs may originate from N-doping of the GQDs which is according to the literature [24]. All performed tests in this part may be a suitable proof of the successful synthesis of the N-GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the morphology of the prepared AuNPs@N-GQDs/GCE
ce:section.ce:para: According to the proposed strategy, the electrodeposition method of the gold substrate leads to the AuNPs grow on the surface of the N-GQDs/GCE. To this purpose, morphologies of the bare GCE, the N-GQDs/GCE and the obtained AuNPs@N-GQDs/GCE surface were studied by SEM technique (Fig. 2). The comparison between recorded SEM images of the bare GCE (Fig. 2E) and the N-GQDs/GCE (Fig. 2F) shows a layer of a nanometer-sized synthesized N-GQDs is immobilized on the GCE electrode surface. Fig. 3F reveal a uniform layer of the AuNPs is successfully electrodeposited onto the N-GQD/GCE surface. According to the proposed strategy, the prepared nanocomposite may amplify sensitivity and facilitate more loading of the Apt molecules onto the AuNPs@N-GQDs/GCE surface as a primary platform. This can be attributed to this fact that the AuNPs have high interactions with biomolecules such as DNA [25] and also, suitable distribution and small size of the synthesized nanoparticles can significantly increase the effective electrode surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical study of the prepared AuNPs@N-GQDs/GCE as the designed aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to demonstrate the feasibility of the proposed amplification strategy, the electrochemical behavior of different modified electrodes in each step was investigated by the recording of related Nyquist curves in [Fe(CN)6]4−/3− solution as an impedance probe, Fig. 3A. According to this technique, the semicircle diameter in the recorded curves is equal to electron transfer (eT) resistance (R
ct ) parameter. The R
ct as a key parameter for the kinetics of a probe at the electrode interface shows the immobilization of the substrates onto the modified electrode surface in each step of the process. In the Fig. 3A, the curve a shows a small semicircle, which is a characteristics feature of the diffusion controlled electrochemical processes. By pipetting some of the N-GQDs on the GCE surface, R
ct value was significantly increased (curve b). It can be attributed to the slight surface immobilization of this sub-layer on the GCE bare. In the curve c, a small semi-circle with a low R
ct indicates a strong eT towards the AuNPs@N-GQDs/GCE, illustrating the successful electrodeposition of the immobilized AuNPs onto the surface of the N-GQDs/GCE. The unique combination of the AuNPs and N-GQDs with its beneficial properties as a nanocomposite led to a high electron transferring ability towards the bare GCE surface. By adding ssDNA1 onto the modified electrode surface with nanocomposite, the R
et value increased (curve d). This is because of the negatively charged phosphate backbone of the DNA oligonucleotides which is led to an electrostatic repulsion force to anion redox probe. In the next step, by hybridization of the ssDNA2 on the ssDNA1/AuNPs@N-GQDs/GCE surface, the R
ct value was drastically increased (curve e). Such a high R
ct value can be attributed to the fact that Apt molecules carrying more negative charges induced electrostatic repulsion between the electrode surface and negatively charged redox species of [Fe(CN)6]4−/3−. In presence of IBP, a large amount of IBP-Apt complexes was obtained on the surface of the proposed aptasensor and the R
ct value was increased (curve f). Apart from the EIS technique, the CV technique was also applied to validate the immobilization of the substrates in each step in the [Fe(CN)6]4−/3− (Fig. 3B) and the RF as a green probe (Fig. 3C). Because the RF is a relatively bulky molecule and has many oxygen atoms, this is considered to have negative partial charge. When the N-GQDs was pipetted on the bare GCE surface (curve a), a low current appeared (curve b). This result is in agreement with the fact that the existence of the electron pair on the N atoms in the structure of the synthesized N-GQDs led to the negative partial charge of this nanomaterial. Thus, the presence of the N-GQDs on the GCE surface led to the repulsion of the negatively charged probe and the eT was decreased. In the next step, by electrodeposition of the AuNPs on the N-GQDs/GCE surface, an increased current response appeared. The remarkable enhancement of the signal amplification in the proposed assay can be due to attachment of a uniform layer of the AuNPs on the N-GQDs/GCE surface (curve c). This current change may be a good proof for successful formation of the AuNPs@N-GQDs nanocomposite on the GCE surface. When the NH2-ssDNA1 was self-assembled onto the AuNPs@N-GQDs/GCE surface, an increase in the peak potential separation and a decrease of the peak current were accrued (curve d). This result confirms the effective immobilization of the amine groups in the ssDNA1 onto the AuNPs on the surface of the AuNPs@N-GQDs/GCE [25]. Afterward, by incubating ssDNA2 on the ssDNA1/AuNPs@N-GQDs/GCE surface and formation of the hybridization reaction between ssDNA2 and ssDNA1 on the modified electrode surface, the eT was significantly decreased. The presence of the ssDNA2 as the Apt molecules comprising 76 nucleotides resulted in the more blocking of the electron flow of the redox probe to the electrode surface (curve e). By incubating IBP as a target on the ssDNA2/ssDNA1/AuNPs@N-GQDs/GCE surface, due to the formation of a large amount of IBP-Apt complexes on the surface and more space restriction, the lower eT value and more peak potential separation were achieved (curve f). These results are in good agreement with those obtained from CV measurements, in which the peak currents varied upon the assembly and binding processes. Both recorded results of EIS and CV confirm that the proposed aptasensor worked indeed as proposed in Scheme 2. The R ct and ∆E = E pa – E pc values related to the modified electrodes in the [Fe(CN)6]4−/3− solution are summarized in Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison between the efficiency of the N-GQDs and GQDs in preparation of the proposed nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para:
To study efficiency effect of the N-GQDs in preparation of a suitable nanocomposite with the high surface area for immobilization of biomolecules, the GQDs as another sub-layer was tested. In this purpose, EIS technique was used for investigation of some electrochemical parameters related to use of both of the N-GQDs/GCE and GQDs/GCE in preparation of the nanocomposite in [Fe(CN)6]4−/3− as an impedance probe. As can be seen in the Fig. 4 , the R
ct value for the N-GQDs on a GCE surface (curve b) is more than the R
ct value for the GQD on the same GCE surface (curve c). It may be related to this fact that due to existing different functional groups in the N-GQDs and GQDs structures, total negative partial charge in the N-GQDs structure is more than total negative partial charge in the GQDs structure. Thus, repulsion between negative charge in the N-GQDs and the impedance probe is more than repulsion between the GQDs and the probe in the same condition. Also, as shown in the inset of the Fig. 4, by electrodeposition of the AuNPs onto the modified electrode surface, the R
ct value for the electrodeposited AuNPs on the N-GQDs/GCE surface (curve e, R
ct = 243 ± 1.5 Ω) is lower than R
ct value for the electrodeposited AuNPs on the GQDs/GCE surface (curve d, R
ct = 870 ± 1.2 Ω). Because of the existence of an affinity between N atoms in the N-GQDs and the AuNPs, this may be a good evidence for confirmation of better electrodeposition of the AuNPs onto the N-GQDs/GCE surface. By more immobilization of the AuNPs on the N-GQDs/GCE surface, more ssDNA1 molecules can covalently attach on the surface and the amplified signal may be achieved which is another advantage of the proposed strategy.

In another investigation, to study the efficiency of the electrodeposited AuNPs on the modified electrode surface, amount of the surface roughness of the used sub-layer on the GCE surface as an active surface area, A (cm−2), was investigated. The A value for the N-GQDs/GCE and the AuNPs/N-GQDs/GCE surfaces were estimated by recording the CVs plots in a 0.1 M PB solution containing 5 mM of [Fe(CN)6]4−/3−according to Randles-Sevcik equation (at different scan rates (ν): 0.01 Vs−1 to 0.1 Vs−1) [26]. The calculated A value for the surface of the N-GQDs/GCE and AuNPs/N-GQDs/GCE were 0.0027 ± 10−4 (cm−2) and 0.0160 ± 5 × 10−4 (cm−2), respectively. So, the use of the AuNPs/N-GQDs as the sub-layer led to an increase of the surface area related to the N-GQDs/GCE. It may be a strong proof that the electrodeposited AuNPs form an efficient platform on the modified electrode surface, causing more surface area, which is capable of more loading of the biomolecules such as DNA onto the modified electrode surface [27].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative and qualitative measurements of the IBP using the designed aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared Apt/AuNPs@N-GQDs/GCE as the proposed aptasensor based on the described experimental results in the previous sections was fabricated to quantify of IBP. Fig. 5A displays DPV calibration curves of the designed aptasensor using 8 different standards concentration of IBP in 0.1 M BP solution (pH = 7.4) containing 1 mM RF solution and 1 mM NaCl as an electrolyte. The relative signal response was exponentially decreased with concentration increasing of IBP which is attributed to the Apt molecules as a capture of IBP molecules participating in the aptasensing assay. The obtained response of the developed aptasensor was found to be linearly proportional to IBP concentration in the range of 0.1 f. to 0.2 μM with two ranges. The linear regression equations are expressed as ΔI (μA) = 5.387 [IBP] (nM) + 0.273 with a correlation coefficient of R2 = 0.992 for low concentration and ΔI (μA) = 0.0017 [IBP] (nM) + 0.273 with a correlation coefficient of R2 = 0.9974 for high concentrations. Limit of quantification (LOQ) of the proposed method is 0.1 fM. Limit of detection (LOD) based on S/N = 3 was calculated to be 33.33 aM and this value is lower than that other reported methods in the literature. Additionally, Table 2 shows some analytical properties such as linear dynamic concentration range and LOD of the easy to use methods which are comparable or better than those of complicated and time-consuming methods.

Some common drugs with similar structure or property with IBP may have an interference effect in this assay. To evaluate of this case, 50 nM of each interfering substances such as COD, DCF, APAP, and valproate sodium (which are 103-fold higher than that of the IBP concentration (50 pM)) were tested. As shown in Fig. 5B, the related ΔI to DPV signal intensity in presence of the drugs is lower than that in the presence of IBP or has no change. The non-specific binding of the interfering substances shows a slight change in comparison with the specific binding of the IBP as the original target. The results suggest that the binding affinity of the IBP to the Apt molecule appears to be much stronger than that of all other substrates and the proposed aptasensing strategy has high specificity for the IBP detection.

To investigate repeatability effect of the developed aptasensor, the related DPV signal to the prepared aptasensor which was incubated with 50 pM of the IBP was recorded and this manner was repeated six times. The obtained data illustrated the relatively high repeatability of the proposed aptasensor. Furthermore, the reproducibility of the designed aptasensor was evaluated to estimate the precision of the aptasensor by using the following method. Six Apt/AuNPs@N-GQDs/GCEs were incubated with 0.1 nM IBP. All the modified electrodes exhibited similar electrochemical responses and the obtained RSD was equal to 2.9%, meaning a high reproducibility of the explained strategy. Furthermore, the stability of the prepared aptasensor as an important factor in fabrication of each sensing device was investigated by measuring current responses of the aptasensor for long-term storage of every 5 days during one month at 4 °C. It retained 95% of its initial response after a storage period of 5 days, revealing the results based on the proposed aptasensor has acceptable stability.

To check the applicability of the fabricated aptasensor, the IBP concentration in real samples was investigated by the standard addition method. Three types of real samples such as pharmaceutical formulation, human blood serum and waste-water containing different concentrations of IBP were analyzed using three independently prepared aptasensors. As shown in Table 3 , the fabricated aptasensor can quantify the IBP with high accuracy and precision within the statistically acceptable error range. Also, the obtained high recovery (97–102%) for the real samples shows the aptasensor can be applied as a model of the clinical tool in the early diagnosis of human diseases.

In comparison with the reported different sensors in IBP detection (Table 2), the successful results for investigation of efficiency of the proposed aptasensor may be ascribed to some important factors: (1) utilizing of the N-GQDs as a sub-layer in preparation of the nanocomposite can create more surface area for immobilization of further substrate on the modified electrode surface, (2) electrodeposition of the AuNPs on the N-GQDs/GCE surface is a simple and fast method for improvement of surface properties, (3) employment of the AuNPs@N-GQDs nanocomposite with some excellent properties including high conductivity of the AuNPs, accelerating eT and high active holes in the AuNPs which is increased the amount of the immobilized NH2-ssDNA1, have been applied for completing of aptasensing assay [27], (4) greatly amplified sensitivity and selectivity were achieved by loading large amounts of the ssDNA2 as a IBP specific Apt, leading to enhanced chance of ssDNA1-ssDNA2 hybridization reaction, (5) more loading of the ssDNA2 on the electrode surface leads to more formation of the IBP-Apt complex and enhance the current response, (6) utilizing of the RF as an active probe in the negative potential range leads to the decrease of the interferences in the proposed aptasensing system. Furthermore, the facile, low-cost and efficient process is another major advantage of this study in comparison with different methods for IBP sensing using special reagents and multi-step amplification processes. Along with all these benefits, there are some disadvantages such as the time required for preparing the modified electrode for attachment of each substrate. However, some methods in Table 2 such as HPLC and Rp-HPLC are accurate and efficient methods but they need a lot of toxic and non-toxic solvents and their selectivity and sensitivity parameters are not so high in comparison with the fabricated aptasensor. In total, the proposed strategy with the described properties can be used in designing of other methods as a suitable model.

In summary, a novel and green approach based on utilizing of the efficient nanocomposite was developed for the IBP detection via aptasensing assay. The N-GQDs's excellent characteristics along with the AuNPs made the nanocomposite as an attractive matrix for covalently attaching of the Apt molecules. By incubation of the IBP on the aptasensor surface, a complex of the Apt and IBP was formed, resulting in an enhanced hindrance of eT reaction of the RF probe on the aptasensor surface. Utilizing the RF as a biological molecule with inherent properties for the first time in measuring of the IBP is another advantage of the proposed strategy. By investigation of the CV and EIS results of the aptasensor, sensitivity, selectivity, and other common important parameters have also been critically accessed and evaluated. The obtained satisfactory results showed appropriate utilizing of the prepared nanocomposite in designing of the aptasensor which is authenticating an excellent practicality in real samples. More importantly, the applied method may become an alternative model for ultrasensitive and facile detection of IBP in the environment, biomedical field, and other applications. It is expected that the proposed strategy can be effective in cheaper and simpler detection of a wide range of targets only by changing of a corresponding Apt molecule.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the support from the Ilam University.
